title: How to Build Stronger Relationships Through Emotional Wellness and Boundaries
guestBio: Vanessa Morgan is a licensed marriage and family therapist with extensive professional experience in communication, conflict resolution, and relationship dynamics. She specializes in helping individuals and couples build deeper, more meaningful connections in both personal and professional settings.
excerpt: Licensed therapist Vanessa Morgan shares expert insights on emotional wellness, setting healthy boundaries, and building stronger relationships. Learn practical strategies for better communication and conflict resolution that work in both personal and professional settings.
metaDescription: Expert therapist Vanessa Morgan shares actionable advice on emotional wellness, boundaries, and building stronger relationships for women leaders.
keyTakeaways: Emotional wellness requires intentional management and grace toward yourself as you navigate life's complexities.
Setting healthy boundaries is essential for maintaining strong relationships in both personal and professional contexts.
Effective communication skills can be learned and applied to resolve conflicts more constructively.
Building deeper connections requires vulnerability and the willingness to engage authentically with others.
Conflict resolution is a skill that strengthens relationships when approached with the right tools and mindset.
Personal growth and emotional intelligence directly impact your ability to lead and influence others.
Professional therapy insights can be practically applied to everyday relationship challenges.

pullQuotes.text: Managing your emotional world with grace and intention is not just possible—it's essential for thriving relationships.

timestamps.label: Vanessa's Background in Marriage and Family Therapy

content:
As women juggling multiple roles—whether you're leading a team, raising children, or building a business—your emotional wellness isn't just a nice-to-have. It's the foundation that everything else stands on. In this powerful conversation with licensed marriage and family therapist Vanessa Morgan, we dive deep into the essential skills that will transform how you navigate relationships in every area of your life.

You know that feeling when you're stretched thin, when every conversation feels like it could go sideways, when you're giving everyone else what they need but feeling depleted yourself? You're not alone, and more importantly, you don't have to stay stuck there. Vanessa brings years of professional experience working with individuals and couples to share insights that go far beyond therapy sessions—these are tools you can use immediately.

The Truth About Emotional Wellness

Emotional wellness isn't about having it all figured out or never feeling overwhelmed. It's about developing the capacity to manage your emotional world with intention and grace. When you're in leadership positions, whether at work or at home, your emotional state ripples out and affects everyone around you. This isn't about perfection—it's about building resilience and emotional intelligence that serves you and those you lead.

Vanessa emphasizes that emotional wellness is a practice, not a destination. You're constantly growing, learning, and adapting. The women who thrive are those who give themselves permission to be human while also taking responsibility for their emotional growth. This means acknowledging when you're struggling, seeking support when you need it, and developing healthy coping mechanisms that actually work.

Boundaries That Actually Work

Let's talk about boundaries—not the guilt-inducing, theoretical kind that make you feel worse about yourself, but practical boundaries that create space for you to show up fully in your relationships. Healthy boundaries aren't walls that keep people out; they're clear guidelines that help you engage authentically while protecting your energy and well-being.

Setting boundaries requires you to know yourself deeply. What drains you? What energizes you? What patterns keep showing up in your relationships that leave you feeling frustrated or resentful? Vanessa guides us through understanding that boundaries are actually gifts we give to our relationships. When you're clear about your limits and communicate them with love and respect, you create space for deeper, more honest connections.

The key is learning to set boundaries without apology while still maintaining warmth and connection. This is especially crucial for women in leadership who often feel pressure to be everything to everyone. You can be generous with your time and energy while still protecting what matters most.

Communication That Builds Connection

Effective communication goes far beyond just talking—it's about creating understanding and connection even in the midst of disagreement. Vanessa shares insights from her work helping couples navigate conflict that apply beautifully to all your relationships, whether you're dealing with a challenging team member, a frustrated client, or tensions at home.

The most powerful communicators understand that being right isn't the goal—being connected is. This shift in perspective changes everything. Instead of entering conversations to prove your point, you enter them to understand and be understood. This doesn't mean you become a pushover or compromise your values. It means you engage from a place of strength and curiosity rather than defensiveness and fear.

Conflict becomes an opportunity for deeper connection when you approach it with the right tools. Learning to listen not just to words but to the emotions and needs underneath them transforms how you handle difficult conversations. You start seeing patterns instead of just reacting to individual incidents.

Building Relationships That Last

Strong relationships—whether personal or professional—are built on a foundation of trust, respect, and genuine care. But they also require skills that many of us were never taught. Vanessa's experience working with couples reveals patterns that show up in all relationships: the importance of showing up consistently, the power of small gestures, and the necessity of addressing issues before they become crises.

Building deeper connections requires vulnerability, but it also requires wisdom about when and how to be vulnerable. Not every relationship can handle the same level of depth, and that's okay. The art is in discerning what each relationship needs and showing up accordingly.

Personal growth isn't selfish—it's one of the greatest gifts you can give to your relationships. When you're doing your own emotional work, when you're learning and growing, you bring more to every interaction. You're less reactive, more present, and better able to see situations clearly rather than through the lens of your own unhealed wounds or unmet needs.

Practical Application in Your Life

The insights Vanessa shares aren't meant to stay in theory—they're meant to change how you show up starting today. Whether you're navigating a challenging conversation with a team member, working through tension in your marriage, or simply wanting to be more present with your children, these principles create transformation when you apply them consistently.

Managing your emotional world with grace and intention is both an art and a skill. It requires practice, patience with yourself, and the willingness to keep learning. But the payoff—stronger relationships, better leadership, and a deeper sense of fulfillment—is worth every bit of effort you invest.

You have everything within you to create the relationships you want. Sometimes you just need the right tools and the permission to use them. This conversation with Vanessa gives you both, along with the encouragement to trust yourself as you navigate the beautiful complexity of human connection.

quiz.title: What's Your Relationship Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally navigate relationships and boundaries in your personal and professional life. Find out what this reveals about your leadership approach and emotional wellness journey.

questions.question: When a conflict arises with someone you care about, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I immediately feel their emotions and want to fix things for everyone involved
I look for ways to smooth things over and find middle ground quickly
I want to address it head-on and get everything out in the open
I need time to think through all the layers before I respond

questions.question: How do you typically handle it when someone asks for help but you're already overwhelmed?

options.text: I usually say yes anyway because I hate disappointing people
I try to find a compromise that works for both of us
I'm honest about my limits and suggest alternatives
I consider their request carefully and weigh all my options before deciding

questions.question: In your leadership style, what do people most often praise you for?

options.text: Being genuinely caring and always making time to listen
Creating a calm, supportive environment where everyone feels comfortable
Being clear about expectations and giving honest feedback
Thinking things through thoroughly and offering thoughtful guidance

questions.question: When you're feeling emotionally drained, what's most likely to restore your energy?

options.text: Having a heart-to-heart conversation with someone I trust
Spending quiet time in a peaceful, organized environment
Doing something physical or tackling a project I can control
Taking time alone to reflect and process my thoughts

questions.question: How do you know when a boundary has been crossed in your relationships?

options.text: I feel emotionally exhausted or resentful, even toward people I love
The peaceful dynamic I work to maintain starts feeling forced or fake
I notice I'm getting more irritated or short-tempered than usual
I start withdrawing or feeling disconnected from the situation

questions.question: What aspect of building stronger relationships feels most challenging for you right now?

options.text: Learning to say no without feeling guilty or selfish
Having difficult conversations without avoiding or sugar-coating
Balancing honesty with sensitivity to others' feelings
Moving from understanding what needs to happen to actually taking action

results.title: The Heart-Led Connector
results.description: You lead with empathy and have a natural gift for creating deep, meaningful relationships. Your emotional intelligence is one of your greatest strengths, though you sometimes struggle with setting boundaries because you care so deeply about others. You have an intuitive understanding of what people need, but you may find yourself depleted when you give too much of yourself. This episode will help you learn how to maintain those beautiful connections while protecting your own emotional wellness and creating sustainable boundaries that actually strengthen your relationships.

results.title: The Steady Harmonizer
results.description: You're the person others turn to when they need stability and peace. You have a natural ability to see all sides of a situation and help others find common ground, but you sometimes avoid difficult conversations to keep the peace. Your strength lies in creating calm, supportive environments, though you might struggle with addressing conflict directly. This episode will give you practical tools for navigating those challenging conversations with confidence while maintaining the harmony you value, helping you grow into an even more effective leader.

results.title: The Direct Communicator
results.description: You value honesty and clarity in all your relationships, and people know exactly where they stand with you. Your straightforward approach is refreshing and builds trust, though sometimes your directness might come across stronger than intended. You're not afraid of difficult conversations, but you may struggle with the emotional nuances that come with them. This episode will help you blend your natural directness with emotional intelligence, giving you tools to communicate with both clarity and compassion for even stronger relationships.

results.title: The Thoughtful Observer
results.description: You're incredibly self-aware and tend to process situations deeply before responding. Your thoughtful approach to relationships means you often see things others miss, and you're skilled at understanding underlying dynamics. However, you might sometimes hold back from fully engaging or wait too long to address issues. This episode will encourage you to trust your insights more and give you practical ways to translate your deep understanding into action, helping you step more boldly into your leadership potential.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in eight figures and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm stoked to kick it off with Vanessa Morgan, an accomplished marriage and family therapist and my wonderful friend and neighbor. Vanessa brings deep expertise to her practice, guiding couples through the ups and downs of their relationships, all the while managing a bustling blended family. We bonded over lunch with our neighbor Chrissy, another therapist, diving into lively chats about love and life and the quirks of men and women. Vanessa's got a quiet grace, striking beauty, and I'm calling it a book waiting to be written.

With chapter one of The Manager in hand, Vanessa, tell me, we've read chapter one and I talk about the invisible labor. What do you find is your most burdensome invisible labor that you have to carry in your house?

**Vanessa:** I think the one for me that I find most burdensome just because it's the most important to me is the emotional labor of trying to think about everyone, especially because I have a blended family. So all the children, my husband, all the different dynamics. I'm trying to help coach these kids about how to advocate for their needs, how to do it in a way that's relational, thinking about how to mentalize how my state is impacting their state. There is so much emotional labor that goes into running a household and it's obviously unseen because it has to do with emotions. So I think for me that's the one that's both nearest and dearest to my heart and the one that I find most challenging and important because there's so many layers and dynamics to it.

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. It's like I never really thought about it, but you're right. Mom's the one who's teaching the kids how to say "I'm frustrated. I'm feeling sad. I'm tired. I'm hungry." How to communicate your needs with people efficiently. We do carry a burden of not just figuring out how our kids are feeling but also helping them to communicate those feelings with the outside world, teaching them how to know what they're feeling.

**Vanessa:** Yes. Know what they're feeling, ask for it, advocate for it, sit with it. There are so many things that we do as moms that are just kind of intuitive that I don't even think get the airtime that they deserve that we are doing all the time behind the scenes. Even in the middle of the night, I wake up thinking, "Oh, I wonder how that was for them or if I need to do this or did I sign up for the thing so they don't feel this way." So yes, a never-ending job.

**Aimee:** Totally. It really is. I want to talk a little bit about our patriarchal vibes. I feel like we all kind of feel that rub at some point in our lives, whether it's with working with the people that we work with in business, with your clients. Where do you get that patriarchal rub, that wound of the patriarchal wound? Where do you feel that?

**Vanessa:** You know, I think for me it's been such a process of awakening that it's almost like the air that we breathe, the water that we swim in. It's so deeply ingrained in us as women to have some... there's so many women that I've seen that have so much unexamined internalized misogyny, where they really don't even know that they're living in this strict idea of who they need to be to be lovable, acceptable, valued in society.

And so I almost want to say there's no facet of life that I think it hasn't touched and that I'm constantly trying to unpack with intentionality how I show up as a woman and how I value myself as a woman. And that's been a really long process that I'm still making meaning of every day.

**Aimee:** Yeah. You know, I think that we've really overlooked the wound conversationally. I think that we've really overlooked the wound in our society. We want to just move forward and be strong women, but I think we've overlooked the fact that women have lived under a patriarchy for 2,000 years. And we were severely prosecuted to the point of being burned at the stake for being a midwife. Because of that social conditioning for thousands of years of social conditioning where women were less than men... Like we really were. We had less rights. We were considered chattel. I mean, we just got the right to vote in this country 100 years ago.

And as we're making this progress, I think that part of it is having these kinds of conversations where we're acknowledging the fact that this wound is still there and that we're still healing this wound and that there's still some real subconscious things that are happening in the minds of women that are an untruth. I was thinking of a tiger that was left in a 10x10 cage and he walked it for 10 years. He walked the edges of his cage and they take him out of his cage and he still walks in that 10 by 10 square shape. And I feel like in some ways women have been set free in this country but we still are walking in that invisible cage.

**Vanessa:** Yes. I think two things. One, there is a learned helplessness that if we have been so conditioned, we don't see the bigger world out there. We don't see that we can step outside of that 10x10 box. So really starting to make that learned helplessness more of a conscious process in our life.

And then the other piece is that when trauma is still going on, it's impossible to heal from it. We can't push it away and just pretend it's not there. We have to give a kind of coherent narrative to the pain that we've experienced or that we continue to experience in order to separate from that trauma and begin the healing process. Because if we're still making ourselves small, not advocating for what it is that we need, we're still largely basing our relating on fear-based principles. I have to be a certain kind of way to be lovable or acceptable or connected to you even. Then there is no real relating. There is no authenticity. There is no intimacy in relationships unless we begin to really give narrative to our suffering.

**Aimee:** You're so right. Yeah, you're so right. And you're right. You can't heal from something when you're in it. Yeah. You really have to almost step out of it in order to begin healing it. And then in the Native American tradition, we say you name the wound. And you first you have to name the wound and you have to honor the wound and then you can heal the wound.

And it's like for me, I think that that process needs to begin in women and we need to name that wound and we need to honor it and know that all the things that we went through actually made us way stronger and all of those gifts and that evolution that has come along with all of that has actually... there are superpowers now. This massive ability to be organizational and managerial, the way that we manage our homes and our families, those are all amazing superpowers that we've picked up along the way. And that was partly created by the wound itself. And so it's like, okay, if we can just name it, honor it, and let's move on. Let's heal this as a collective. I'd love to heal this wound for women on this planet. You know, I feel like that once we can heal, women can actually reach their full potential.

**Vanessa:** Yes. I think there's something so beautiful about not being afraid of our shadow or being afraid of the pain that we've suffered and being able to talk about it and share our experience with other people. I think that is healing.

**Aimee:** A thousand percent. Totally. So this is more techy but okay. So there are so many new apps. I mean since COVID I feel like smartphones are like a whole other animal now and it's a new app... For me I probably get a new app almost once a month but managing your house, what are a couple of apps that you use that are lifesavers for you?

**Vanessa:** I mean the main one that I use every day for almost every facet of my life from work to planning to meal prep is Chat GPT. I love Chat GPT. I will talk to it about hey what should I do about this and even if it doesn't give me all the answers even just having kind of a place that organizes my thinking or reflects it back to me I think it's a very helpful tool to have as a mom woman that runs the household.

**Aimee:** It's such a... I love that. I love Life 360 because I like to know where all my people are. Life 360 is a game changer especially with teenagers. It's like the best thing ever invented and I was so resistant for so long and now we're on it and it's totally changed my game because now I can just go, "Oh, hey, where's Julian? Oh, where's Gabriel?" Okay, they're fine, and it gives me such peace of mind just knowing that then I know where they are at all times.

**Vanessa:** Yes. If I want to know, it's... I don't have to call them and hope they answer the phone. It's just boop, look them up and see right where they are. And you feel like as a mom, a big part of our brain is actually dedicated to kid GPS. And so I feel like this Life 360 is such an extension of that female brain in some ways.

**Aimee:** Yes, you... Yeah, totally. And I... So how are you using Chat GPT for meal tracking? I'm curious about this.

**Vanessa:** So I will just speak into it. Here's what I had to eat today from this restaurant. How many calories is it? How many grams of protein have I gotten today? How many more do I need? What are some ideas about how I could get that?

**Aimee:** Wow. I didn't even know you could do that. I love that. That's such a creative way to use it. Yeah. To make sure you get your macros. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Protein. You need a protein. You got to get your protein. I love that. And then so what I'm just curious, this is just spitballing this one, but tell me on a daily basis, what are you aiming for as far as grams of protein and from a nutrition standpoint? What are your nutrition goals as far as your vitamins and your protein? What are you looking for?

**Vanessa:** I really have felt such a difference when I do incorporate... the supplements that I take, I feel like I've gotten it down. It's been so much trial and error. For me, iodine has really changed the way that my... feeling my body, my energy levels. That's only been in the last year or two. My magnesium. I love my magnesium for sleep. My B vitamins. So my supplements and then protein. I really try to frontload my day with a lot of protein. Start off with a protein shake.

**Aimee:** How big? How many grams do you go?

**Vanessa:** 20 grams in the morning. 25 grams in the morning. And then pretty much every meal period, I'm at least trying to get 25 grams. So, a little bit over 100 every day.

**Aimee:** I love that because I wasn't doing that and I could just feel my muscles kind of atrophying and I was having to work so much harder to maintain the muscle that was so easy to do in my youth. And so that has been as I get older something that's been very valuable to me.

**Vanessa:** Totally. Yeah. I've always been a big protein girl, but I love... My brain just needs it. I need it to think. And I do the collagen protein now, and the byproduct of collagen protein is hair. Yes. Yes. Hair and nails.

**Aimee:** Yes. Okay. Works for me. And then we've talked about this. You have a book brewing in you. I know you do. But tell me a little bit about what you're thinking. Where what do you think your book will be about?

**Vanessa:** So, I've learned as a couple therapist. I've been doing this for 15 years, really intently studying couples and relationships for the last decade. I want to talk about what I've learned in my own relationships because that's had its own story arc to it, has its own life of its own. And then what I've learned from seeing and working with other couples.

And so I want to teach what I've learned about the skills that you really need. Empathy, self-reflection, how to slow things down, curiosity, compassion, how to create a shared vision with someone. What kind of life do we want to have? How do we need to have each other's backs in this world so that we can co-create something? So, I'm really passionate and not just romantic relationships, but the sisterhood relationships. There's so many... I think relationships that people actually need to feel like a fully functioning human.

**Aimee:** You're so right.

**Vanessa:** So I want to teach people about... I think that is my life's work is to teach people how to have the skills that they need to be successful in relationships because it's the number one thing actually linked to our happiness and longevity is the quality of the relationships that you have in your life.

**Aimee:** Totally. We kind of have such a monetarily based society that we forget that our happiness actually doesn't come from money. Statistically speaking, the more money you have, it's not the happier you are. It's the better quality of relationships you have. When our nervous systems were still developing, when we were 1 to 5 years old, we didn't know about money. We didn't understand the construct of money and our happiness was just based on the quality of the connections around us. And I think if we return to that as a society even, we're going to have so much more value in our lives.

**Vanessa:** Oh, a thousand percent. Yeah. I love that. I love that.

**Aimee:** And I love that. I really do. I see that in you and I saw that right away when you're the new neighbor and you're like, "Hey, let's go get lunch," and we've kind of started our little lunch club and it was... you really want to make connection, real connections with the people that you're interfacing with. And I think that that's just admirable actually in our society today because it's not something that people are prioritizing in general.

**Vanessa:** We don't even know we need it. We don't even know how much we need it. Just to have someone down the street that you admire and you can count on and you can be inspired by them when you talk to them. That to me that's life.

**Aimee:** Yeah. No, you're so right. Yeah. No, it is really what fills us up. It fills up your little heart cup. Just to know that you're surrounded by people that you really know. Otherwise, you're living in a neighborhood or you're living in a world with a bunch of strangers. And that's a lonely place to be because this is a really crowded world. And if you don't really know anybody, you can really feel even more isolated.

**Vanessa:** Yeah, absolutely. I mean, you can be around people and feel so lonely if you don't have the skills to connect with them.

**Aimee:** A thousand percent. Yeah. And I feel like we're definitely living in that kind of world right now where people are really lacking those skills.

**Vanessa:** Yeah. I really worry that one potential outcome of humanity is that there is this population collapse because we don't have what we need to be relational. And if we stay in this power over model where there's all this oppression and we're not in a relational model where we're working together... I worry about the outcome of that. We're all just going to have boyfriend girlfriend AI bots and not be procreating. I don't want to put that out there and get too doom and gloom, but I do want to stress the importance of... I think we should really take these skills serious.

**Aimee:** No, I think you're a thousand percent right. Yeah. I think we've really undervalued the ability just to connect with each other and we're so... it's so... we're so many distractions now. It's so easy to tune out. It's so easy to avoid connecting with people. We can just pretend like we're connecting with people. We can go on social media and scroll around and look at what other people are doing and feel like we're connected to other people, but we're not.

**Vanessa:** Yeah. We get some of the same dopamine hits and some of the same oxytocin hits, but it's cheap. It's not lasting.

**Aimee:** Right. Totally. And as the world progresses towards this virtual space, these kinds of skills are going to be extremely valuable for people and it's really going to be what will hopefully end up helping us to create a cooperative relational future for humanity where we are actually connected to one another and we're actually in relationships with each other and we're actually truly happy as a species.

**Vanessa:** Yes. Right. Instead of "if I just get that Ferrari, I'll be happy."

**Aimee:** Yes. Well, it's not fear based. Right. Right. So many of these things, if I have this external thing that I'm going to feel different than I feel and I'm afraid of how I'm feeling right now. But if we're connected with other people, it's not a fear-based way of living in the world.

**Vanessa:** Yes. I love that. Yeah. Because a lot of it's overcoming all of the commercials and all of the advertising that's coming at us that's trying... that's playing on our fear and trying to sell us something. And so we're being used. We're being used by all of these social media platforms and they're working on our fear and basically making us even more disconnected by taking away that time that we would be spending with actual people, and so it's widening that gap.

**Aimee:** I think it's an unwise use of our collective energy a thousand percent.

**Vanessa:** Yes, we can do better.

**Aimee:** Yes. Yes, we will do better. Yeah. I love that. I love that so much. What's next for you? What's your next big move? What are you focused on right now?

**Vanessa:** I feel like this phase of my life really is about balance that I do have pre-teen, teens at home and I really want to usher them into adulthood in a way that I feel like I give it everything. You know, I want to give them my A+ mom effort. So that's my biggest priority still right now. But I can't give my full mom effort if I'm not tending to also the thing that makes me feel so full of passion and excitement, which is this mission that I have. I feel that I have to kind of spread these ideas about relating. So, I want to find... I don't know if I'm going to write a program or a book. I don't know if I'm going to use some technology. I don't really know. I'm still in the creative wondering phase.

**Aimee:** Whatever you're going to do, we're going to be there with you. We want to know what you're doing. We want to be a part of it. So, yeah, we're going to pre-order that book or that program. The sisterhood's right here. The Manager sisterhood is here for you. We want to see what's next. You have a wonderful gift to the world and we want to see all you. We want to see you shine.

**Vanessa:** And that means so much to me. I think that there is something so healing about that sisterhood, really being invited into greatness by another woman is such a treasure. You know, it's something that... there's space for you. We don't have to compete. Come here. I see you. I see the greatness in you. I'm inspired by you. That is such healing for the feminine collective.

**Aimee:** Oh, a thousand percent. And it's so true. I feel like empowered women empower women. And it's like if we can all... if I can help one woman rise and she can help one woman rise and we can all just rise up and rise into our power as women and give our gifts to the world because we really have important messages. God works through us in different ways and we have such important work to do here.

**Vanessa:** I know we all really do. Yeah.

**Aimee:** Well, next question. What's your secret weapon in your house?

**Vanessa:** My secret weapon, I mean, I feel like a broken record today about pumping, really pumping out these idea of relationships, but I do think my secret weapon is learning to be kind of mindful, intentional in all the relationships that I have really see how can I be with this person in a way that creates something new between us in any given moment? What can we create together? Or how can I be with them to learn something new or see them in a different way or be seen by them in a different way.

So I think my superpower is kind of how I think about the world. I try to think about things from many different angles, not just be stuck in this one kind of linear way of thinking about things, but put on a lot of different hats, look from a lot of different angles, and really open up this space of curiosity. That feels like a superpower to me.

**Aimee:** It truly is a superpower, actually. And it's a really awesome perspective for women to have because I feel like so many of us do it but we don't even realize we're doing it. And it's also a skill that we really can work on. Once we're aware of it, it's like, "Oh, I'm good at that, but maybe I could be better." It's an intention of really... It's a mindfulness practice of, "Oh, I'm noticing this is my first impulse, my first thought, my first feeling, and then what comes next?" It's a wondering that kind of invites more in.

**Vanessa:** Absolutely.

**Aimee:** And I just want to really thank you for your time today. It's been so awesome to talk with you and get your perspective on relationships and how we can make the world a better place. I think that you have such a beautiful vision for the world.

**Vanessa:** Oh well, thank you Aimee. It's been such a pleasure to be here and I'm so excited and energized and rejuvenated by the work that you're doing. So thank you for having me.

**Aimee:** Yeah, you're so welcome. I'm so glad that we had this time to talk with each other and do this work because it's work. It really is.

**Vanessa:** It is work. It's important work. It's fun.

**Aimee:** Yes. But work doesn't always have to be hard. And many hands make light work, right?

**Vanessa:** Exactly. Yeah. It's like when you're following your passion, you really... It doesn't feel like work.

**Aimee:** Yes. And it's like we can all change the world by just giving our little gift and sharing it with our sisters. Seeing other sisters that are doing similar work is just... it's fuel. It's so exciting.

**Vanessa:** Totally.

**Aimee:** Well, thank you. Thank you. Thank you so much. And I really appreciate you. Hope to have you back soon.

**Vanessa:** Thank you for having me.

**Aimee:** Yeah.
